records.morph: [imp. Wreathed (?); p. p. Wreathed; Archaic Wreathen (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Wreathing.]

defs: To cause to revolve or writhe; to twist about; to turn.
quotes: And from so heavy sight his head did wreathe.

defs: To twist; to convolve; to wind one about another; to entwine.
quotes: The nods and smiles of recognition into which this singular physiognomy was wreathed.
From his slack hand the garland wreathed for Eve Down dropped.

defs: To surround with anything twisted or convolved; to encircle; to infold.
quotes: Each wreathed in the other's arms.
Dusk faces with withe silken turbants wreathed.
And with thy winding ivy wreathes her lance.

defs: To twine or twist about; to surround; to encircle.
quotes: In the flowers that wreathe the sparkling bowl, Fell adders hiss.

defs: To be intewoven or entwined; to twine together; as, a bower of wreathing trees.
